summary:
*Lord of the Seventh. Lord of the Flies. Lord of Lies. The Slug Archduke. The Fallen One. Prince of Maladomini. The Perfect Ruin.*

Baalzebul is the archdevil of lies, flies, corruption, bureaucracy, ruined ambition, diseased perfection, polluted order, decay hidden under ornament, and the eternal failure of pride to make itself pure. He rules the seventh Hell, Maladomini: a realm of collapsing cities, poisoned archives, unfinished palaces, broken avenues, fetid canals, abandoned monuments, and plans so vast that no completion could ever satisfy the mind that conceived them.

Where Glasya makes corruption agile, Baalzebul makes corruption administrative. Where Mammon prices the soul, Baalzebul falsifies the record by which the price is remembered. Where Asmodeus embodies infernal law as final sovereignty, Baalzebul embodies the lie that law, hierarchy, and refinement can be made perfect if enough inconvenient truths are buried beneath procedure.

He is not falsehood alone. Truth can wound, conceal, enlighten, condemn, or save, and a lie can sometimes shield the innocent from cruelty. Baalzebul is falsehood enthroned as system. He is the forged report, the corrected genealogy, the witness coached into certainty, the archive cleansed of defeat, the public work begun to hide the failure of the last public work, the official beauty stretched across a rotting foundation.

His evil is grand, filthy, and ashamed of its own filth. He yearns for magnificence and leaves ruin. He hungers for reverence and breeds disgust. He demands order and produces labyrinths of excuse. He cannot bear imperfection, yet everything he touches becomes evidence against him.

Baalzebul is the archdevil as fallen angel, disgraced prince, supreme bureaucrat, court liar, ruined architect, patron of corrupt officials, master of infernal archives, prince of decay, and sovereign of the lie repeated until it becomes the only map anyone is allowed to use.
tax:
Baalzebul is a unique archdevil and the sovereign ruler of the seventh Hell. He belongs beneath the Archdevils pantheon as one of Hell’s great office-bearing powers, whose authority is bound to Maladomini, to infernal bureaucracy, and to the metaphysics of corruption disguised as order.

His classification rests on six defining qualities: archdevil status, infernal sovereignty, lawful evil alignment, rulership of Maladomini, dominion over lies and flies, and a portfolio centered on decay, corrupted administration, diseased ambition, and ruined perfection.

Baalzebul is not merely a devil who lies. Lesser fiends deceive for appetite, advantage, survival, or amusement. Baalzebul governs falsehood as civic structure. He rules the office where the truth is misfiled, the ministry where guilt is reassigned, the court where every witness agrees to the official version, the archive where altered records become more durable than memory.

He is also not merely a patron of filth. Flies, rot, sludge, crawling vermin, and polluted air are not decorative horrors around him. They are theological statements. They reveal what happens when corruption is hidden instead of cleansed. The fly is witness, scavenger, herald, and clerk of decay. It gathers where the beautiful surface has failed to conceal the dead thing beneath.

His origin as a fallen celestial power is central to his nature. He is not low wickedness rising upward, but high brilliance collapsing inward. He knows beauty, order, obedience, light, hierarchy, and service well enough to counterfeit them exquisitely. His fall is not the abandonment of perfection, but the attempt to possess perfection as personal glory.

Baalzebul is distinct from Dispater, though both are masters of structure. Dispater builds secure systems to control danger. Baalzebul builds elaborate systems to conceal failure. Dispater fears breach. Baalzebul fears exposure.

He is distinct from Mammon, though both are corrupting powers of civic life. Mammon asks what can be bought. Baalzebul asks what can be made official. Mammon purchases witnesses. Baalzebul edits the testimony until the witnesses believe themselves honest.

He is distinct from Mephistopheles, though both possess towering pride and rivalrous ambition. Mephistopheles burns with intellectual defiance and cold infernal genius. Baalzebul decays beneath the weight of his own self-justifying record. Mephistopheles wishes to surpass the throne. Baalzebul wishes the archive to prove he was always meant to sit nearer it.

Older stems around his name carry the odor of fly-lords, false gods of refuse, adversaries of truth, pestilence spirits, carrion-kings, and the mocked majesty of a prince whose court is attended by vermin. These strands are not separate masks. They are the same humiliation seen from different angles: lordship persisting after glory has curdled.
ap:
Baalzebul appears in several forms, each carrying a different stage of his disgrace.

In his most hated form, he is the Slug Archduke: vast, glistening, swollen, and unspeakably foul, with the remnants of princely majesty embedded in a body of slime, folds, pallid flesh, and crawling flies. His head may retain infernal nobility, terrible intelligence, and a voice like poisoned honey, but the body beneath is a sentence written in living humiliation. Jewels sink into him. Robes cling wetly to him. Crowns seem both magnificent and obscene when set above such ruin.

In his restored or idealized form, he appears as a radiant fallen prince: tall, golden, beautiful, sharp-eyed, and terrible, with wings darkened by pride, regal garments of black, amber, green-gold, and tarnished white, and a face too perfect to trust. This form is not innocence recovered. It is a lie so refined that it almost persuades reality to apologize.

Between these extremes, Baalzebul may appear as a handsome infernal noble surrounded by flies, a robed magistrate whose skin gleams faintly with slime, a winged angelic ruin with compound-eyed shadows, a crowned figure whose perfume cannot hide decay, or a vast monarch of corruption enthroned amid damp ledgers and collapsing marble.

His presence changes the air first. A sweet smell turns sour. Ink thickens. Fruit spots with rot. Paper softens at the edges. Polished stone sweats. Flies appear one by one, then by the hundred, each too attentive to be natural. Voices become uncertain about what they just said. Official records seem more trustworthy than living witnesses. Beauty remains visible, but the eye begins to notice stains beneath it.

Common signs of his influence include documents whose corrections bleed through later pages, witnesses repeating identical phrases without conspiracy, statues of perfect rulers covered in flyspecks, maps of cities that no longer exist, fountains clogged with black larvae, seals impressed over scraped parchment, and grand avenues that lead only to unfinished monuments.

In monstrous revelation, Baalzebul’s forms converge. The glorious prince splits and leaks. The slug opens eyes like old suns. The flies speak in courtly grammar. The lie becomes beautiful and disgusting at once, and the witness understands that decay is not the opposite of his majesty. It is the truth of it.
eco:
Baalzebul has no natural ecology of birth, brood, or animal range. His ecology is bureaucratic decay. He flourishes wherever institutions preserve their own image more fiercely than their purpose.

His natural habitats are corrupt ministries, ruined capitals, unfinished monuments, archives of altered records, diseased courts, civic planning halls, public works offices, false tribunals, propaganda houses, abandoned palaces, polluted canals, tax registries, plague offices, state temples, collapsed libraries, censorial bureaus, and any chamber where officials decide what the truth must have been.

Maladomini, under Baalzebul, is a realm of impossible administrative ruin. It is full of cities begun in pursuit of perfection and abandoned when perfection failed to flatter their maker. Towers crumble beside new foundations. Roads are surveyed through districts no one inhabits. Archives swell with contradictory decrees. Canals stink beneath triumphal bridges. Palaces stand empty except for devils arguing over jurisdiction. Every reform becomes another ruin before the last ruin is honestly named.

The seventh Hell is not chaotic. That is its horror. It is meticulously mismanaged. Every collapse has paperwork. Every polluted district has a committee. Every abandoned city has a commemorative plaque explaining why it was necessary to begin the next one. Every failure is archived as progress toward a more perfect design.

Baalzebul spreads by falsification rather than conquest.

One official changes a date to protect a superior.
One court accepts the amended record because scandal would harm public confidence.
One architect conceals a failed foundation beneath ornament.
One priest explains that visible rot is a test of faith.
One minister declares the ruined quarter a completed phase.
One scholar cites the false report.
One generation inherits the lie as history.
In time, truth becomes treason against continuity.

His influence favors societies with heavy bureaucracy, public grandeur, legal formalism, censorial offices, perfectionist rulers, official histories, massive public works, corrupted courts, urban decay, institutional pride, propaganda ministries, hereditary offices, and civic shame so deep that no one dares name the failure everyone can smell.

Yet Baalzebul is not confined to vast governments. He enters guilds through falsified accounts, temples through altered miracle records, families through edited ancestry, military companies through false casualty reports, schools through corrected doctrine, and adventuring bands through the first agreed lie told to make a victory look cleaner than it was.

His most fertile environments are not places where everyone lies casually. They are places where everyone knows which lies must remain official.
beh:
Baalzebul is proud, brilliant, resentful, fastidious, persuasive, ashamed, vindictive, and endlessly self-justifying. He hates ugliness, especially when it reveals him. He hates failure, especially when it is his. He hates truth most deeply when truth is inelegant.

His temptations often begin with reputation.

He offers the ruler an official history that preserves legitimacy.
He offers the official a way to hide one necessary alteration.
He offers the architect a chance to erase a failed design.
He offers the priest a doctrine that explains corruption without requiring repentance.
He offers the scholar access to records no one else may see.
He offers the criminal a forged innocence recognized by every court.
He offers the defeated general a revised report and a second command.
He offers the liar the comfort of becoming believed.

Baalzebul rarely says, “Lie because lies are good.” He says the truth is incomplete, dangerous, premature, destabilizing, vulgar, unfair to the larger plan, harmful to morale, disrespectful to office, exploitable by enemies, or technically inaccurate in a way only experts can correct. He teaches mortals to lie with clean hands and procedural language.

His servants are often administrators rather than obvious villains: clerks, magistrates, ministers, scribes, city planners, archivists, censors, heralds, architects, auditors, temple lawyers, public historians, urban priests, and court devils who know how to make a lie look exhausted by paperwork.

Toward servants, Baalzebul rewards usefulness, elegance, discretion, and the ability to produce a record that survives scrutiny. He despises sloppiness. A crude liar is beneath him. A brilliant falsifier who can make fraud appear to be the only responsible interpretation may be elevated highly. Yet all his servants live under the risk of becoming scapegoats. Baalzebul’s systems require blame to travel downward.

Toward enemies, he prefers reputational annihilation, procedural delay, falsified evidence, poisoned testimony, historical erasure, humiliation through exposure, and the slow rot of confidence. He may destroy a foe’s body, but he takes greater pleasure in making the foe’s own allies unable to prove what was done to them.

Toward other archdevils, Baalzebul is ambitious and bitter. His rivalry with the greatest powers of Hell is sharpened by memory of disgrace. He knows that he has been mocked, punished, reduced, restored, and watched. This has not humbled him. It has made him more intricate. He plots with the patience of a being who understands that a forged record can outlive a defeated army.

His central vice is not lying alone. It is the refusal to let truth limit pride.
traitsText:
**Tags:** Unique, Deity, Archdevil, Devil, Fiend, Lawful, Evil, Extraplanar, Immortal, Divine, Air, Earth, Poison, Disease, Fear, Mental, Charm, Compulsion, Contract, Oath, Lies, Flies, Decay, Bureaucracy, Corruption, Pride, Tyranny, Hell

**Alignment** lawful evil
**Portfolio** lies, flies, decay, corruption, bureaucracy, ruined perfection, false records, poisoned cities, diseased ambition, institutional rot
**Domains** Evil, Law, Trickery, Nobility, Earth
**Subdomains** Devil, Deception, Espionage, Legislation, Leadership, Toil, Tyranny, Decay, Plague, Ambition
**Favored Weapon** spear, heavy mace, staff, or hooked scepter
**Symbol** a fly’s head, a web heavy with flies, a crowned slug, or a black seal stamped over scraped parchment
**Sacred Colors** tarnished gold, black, bile-green, ivory, amber, and bruised purple
**Favored Servants** devils, corrupt bureaucrats, censors, propagandists, false witnesses, diseased priests, archivists, city planners, failed architects, court liars, plague-bearing servants, ambitious officials, and rulers who hide decay beneath public grandeur

Baalzebul’s lawful trait is expressed through records, courts, offices, seals, permits, archives, decrees, reports, official histories, planning commissions, inspection rites, and procedural falsehood. He does not attack truth only by denying it. He buries it in approved forms until denial becomes unnecessary.

His evil lies in the preservation of corruption for the sake of pride. He teaches that institutions must survive even when their purpose has rotted, that official beauty is more important than living health, that an elegant lie is superior to a humiliating truth, and that decay should be managed as a public image problem rather than cured.

Air belongs to him through flies, buzzing rumors, polluted breath, whispered reports, and the pestilent movement of lies from mouth to mouth. Earth belongs to him through ruins, foundations, mud, buried records, abandoned cities, and the heavy civic weight of failed ambition. Poison and disease belong to him as corruption given body.

His fear, mental, charm, and compulsion traits arise from reputation, bureaucracy, and repeated falsehood. He frightens by threatening exposure, charms by offering official validation, and compels through the pressure of systems that make truth costly to speak and nearly impossible to prove.

Contract and oath are central to his power because lies are most dangerous when recorded under seal. Testimony, civic charters, altered reports, false affidavits, forged genealogies, censored archives, inspection certificates, and official histories become his scripture when used to sanctify corruption.
abilities:
**Aura of Official Falsehood (Su)**
Baalzebul radiates an aura in which lies feel responsible, records feel more trustworthy than memory, and exposure feels socially dangerous. Lawful evil creatures gain confidence in deception, procedure, and reputational control. Others may hesitate to contradict official accounts, even when they know those accounts are false. This is a mind-affecting charm and compulsion effect.

**Lordship of Maladomini (Su)**
Baalzebul commands the seventh Hell’s ruins, archives, polluted canals, unfinished cities, civic offices, fly-swarms, waste pits, and decaying monuments. In Maladomini, buildings may alter their plans, roads may lead to abandoned projects, archives may bury the needed record beneath endless approvals, and flies may gather wherever a lie has begun to rot.

**Infernal Falsification (Sp)**
Baalzebul may alter records, memories, testimony, signatures, seals, maps, genealogies, decrees, and official histories through compact. The alteration need not be perfect at first. His art lies in making the falsehood administratively durable: difficult to challenge, embarrassing to question, and supported by enough secondary documents that truth appears irresponsible.

**The Lie Becomes Procedure (Su)**
A falsehood accepted by an institution under Baalzebul’s influence can become harder to correct each time it is repeated in official form. Reports cite reports. Witnesses adjust memory to match records. Courts hesitate to overturn precedent. Ministers protect the institution by protecting the lie. Over time, correction feels like rebellion.

**Lord of the Flies (Su)**
Baalzebul commands flies, carrion insects, rot-swarms, and verminous witnesses of decay. His flies may spy, spread disease, obscure sight, carry whispers, mark guilty flesh, consume documents selectively, or gather around concealed corruption. The smallest fly may be a clerk of Maladomini.

**Slug Archduke’s Curse (Su)**
Baalzebul’s humiliation is also a weapon. He may inflict transformations that reveal inward corruption through outward degradation: noble flesh becoming soft and pale, tongues swelling with lies, limbs losing grace under the weight of indulgence, perfume turning to stench, or beauty becoming too moist, too swollen, too alive with flies. Such curses are especially potent against proud deceivers.

**Tongue of the Fallen Prince (Su)**
Baalzebul’s speech can make falsehood sound like wounded dignity. He excels at half-truth, careful omission, legal phrasing, strategic confession, and the lie that hides behind a true grievance. Even when bound to speak truth in some manner, he may arrange truths so that listeners damn themselves by inference.

**Archive of Contradictions (Su)**
Baalzebul can preserve mutually incompatible records without allowing their contradiction to weaken his system. Each document is useful in a different court, under a different seal, or against a different enemy. His servants learn to ask not what is true, but which record has jurisdiction.

**Pestilent Perfection (Su)**
Baalzebul can inspire rulers, architects, artists, priests, and officials with visions of perfect cities, perfect doctrines, perfect laws, perfect genealogies, and perfect reputations. The vision is magnificent and impossible. Each failed attempt produces ruin, waste, cruelty, and new excuses to begin again. This ability is most dangerous to the brilliant and ashamed.

**Web of Testimony (Su)**
Baalzebul can bind witnesses into a mutually reinforcing structure of lies. Each witness may know only part of the deception, but all become invested in preserving it. A single contradiction threatens everyone, so everyone becomes guard to everyone else’s falsehood. The web catches flies, but it also catches clerks, judges, priests, and kings.

**Corruption Made Visible (Su)**
When Baalzebul wishes, hidden rot becomes physically manifest: mold blooming beneath paint, flies gathering around forged documents, statues sweating black ichor, wounds smelling of spoiled honey, or a palace wall splitting to reveal waste behind marble. He uses revelation not for repentance, but for humiliation, blackmail, and proof that only he knows how deep the rot goes.

**Seal of Revised Truth (Sp)**
Baalzebul may stamp a document, memory, verdict, or public narrative with infernal authority. For a time, those under the relevant institution treat the revised version as valid. The seal does not make the lie metaphysically true, but it gives the lie standing. Challengers must fight not only falsehood, but the authority that has adopted it.

**City of Failed Plans (Su)**
Baalzebul may curse cities, temples, guilds, or courts with endless improvement that never heals the original wound. Buildings are demolished for grander replacements. Laws are rewritten but not made just. Offices are reorganized but not cleansed. Public works consume lives and treasure while decay spreads beneath them. The institution becomes addicted to reform as a substitute for repentance.

**Scapegoat Descent (Su)**
Baalzebul can redirect blame downward through hierarchy. A ruler’s lie becomes a minister’s miscommunication; a minister’s crime becomes a clerk’s error; a clerk’s obedience becomes personal guilt. This ability does not erase responsibility before higher judgment, but it makes mortal institutions punish the least protected bearer of the record.

**Carrion Crown (Su)**
Baalzebul may empower disgraced officials, fallen priests, ruined nobles, and exposed liars with renewed authority if they embrace corruption openly enough to become useful. The crown does not cleanse shame. It makes shame contagious. Followers gather not because they believe the ruler innocent, but because shared guilt becomes a political body.

**The Perfect Excuse (Sp)**
Baalzebul can offer an explanation that preserves pride at the cost of truth. The excuse may be elegant, plausible, and partly accurate. It allows a creature to continue a harmful course without admitting failure. Each acceptance of such an excuse makes later honesty more painful and therefore less likely.

**Rot Beneath the Record (Su)**
Where Baalzebul’s cult or influence dominates, the physical environment begins to resemble the moral record: clogged drains, fly-ridden courts, damp archives, cracked monuments, sour food at state banquets, sickly officials, and public buildings that look magnificent from the front while collapsing behind closed doors. The realm itself becomes a document no censor can fully edit.
bg:
Baalzebul’s legend is a fall from brightness into filth.

He is remembered as Triel, a glorious celestial power whose gifts were real: beauty, intelligence, command, and a vision of order magnificent enough to draw admiration even from enemies. His fall did not begin with stupidity or crude appetite. It began with ambition refined into grievance. He believed himself fit for higher station, deserving of greater rule, worthy of honor beyond what had been granted.

Hell welcomed that belief and perfected it.

Under Asmodeus, Triel became Baalzebul: a prince whose brilliance served tyranny, a fallen one whose hunger for recognition became infernal policy. He rose high, commanded greatly, and became one of the most infamous powers beneath the throne. His ambition did not end with elevation. It sharpened. No rank could satisfy the inward claim that he deserved more.

His disgrace is central to his myth. Baalzebul conspired against the order above him and failed. His punishment was not simple destruction. He was made grotesque. The magnificent prince became the Slug Archduke, a lord of flies and filth, forced to rule amid the visible vocabulary of his own corruption. Hell did not merely defeat him. It made his body into commentary.

Yet the punishment did not remove his power. This is one of Hell’s crueler lessons. Disgrace, if useful, may be institutionalized. Baalzebul remained lord. His humiliation became part of his authority. His servants learned that corruption could survive exposure if the office endured.

Maladomini reflects him perfectly. It was built and rebuilt by the logic of impossible improvement. Each abandoned city, each ruined avenue, each vast unfinished work, each polluted district, each archive of contradictory law bears the mark of a ruler who cannot admit that the flaw lies in the will directing the project. He seeks a perfect capital and produces a geography of excuses.

His cults often arise among the ambitious who have failed publicly or who fear failing: ministers, scholars, architects, propagandists, judges, priests, urban reformers, disgraced nobles, and rulers who believe their vision is too important to be judged by ordinary truth. They are not always lazy or incompetent. Many are brilliant. Many see real disorder. Many begin with legitimate plans.

Baalzebul corrupts them by making failure unbearable.

A good ruler can admit an error.
A Baalzebulan ruler must rename it.
A good architect can rebuild a foundation.
A Baalzebulan architect must bury the inspection report.
A good priest can confess corruption.
A Baalzebulan priest must write doctrine explaining why the smell is holy.

Baalzebul is defeated not by mocking his ugliness or denying his intelligence. He is defeated by truthful humility, public accountability, repair without vanity, records that serve justice rather than reputation, and the courage to leave a ruin standing long enough to learn from it.
note:
Baalzebul is most effective as the patron of official decay: the infernal power behind falsified records, corrupt bureaucracies, failed utopias, poisoned cities, rewritten histories, public grandeur over private rot, and leaders who would rather build a new monument than admit the old one is collapsing.

His influence may appear through:

a city whose public plans are perfect and whose streets are unlivable

an archive where the true record exists but has been made procedurally unreachable

a ruler who begins new public works whenever old failures become visible

a court where everyone knows the testimony was altered but no one can prove which version came first

a temple that explains plague as divine favor because admitting negligence would ruin its authority

an architect who sacrifices workers to preserve the appearance of flawless design

a censor who believes truth must be delayed until society is strong enough to survive it

a disgraced minister restored to office because everyone else is implicated in the same lie

a swarm of flies that gathers only around documents touched by corruption

a fallen celestial relic that grows more beautiful as the city around it rots

Baalzebul’s stories should not treat him merely as a grotesque monster. His horror depends on the contrast between magnificence and decay. He should be intelligent, refined, wounded, persuasive, and genuinely capable of recognizing disorder. His plans may be beautiful on parchment. His servants may speak in the language of reform, continuity, stability, and public confidence.

The corruption lies in the refusal to let truth interrupt the image.

He works best where institutions matter: courts, archives, temples, cities, ministries, guilds, universities, armies, and noble houses. His plots should involve documents, reputation, civic projects, hidden rot, blame, inspection, and the terrible question of whether exposing the truth will harm people who depend on the lie.

The question Baalzebul poses is not, “Will you lie?”

It is, “What truth must be corrected, so the perfect design can survive?”

And then the flies gather where the correction was made.
